other_observers: Susan Allerton and other Durango birders.; Susan Allerton
specific_location: To be provided later, as the landowner wishes the location to not be disclosed.
description: These were slim spizella sparrows that appeared long-tailed in flight. The body of the birds was medium gray. The underparts were slightly lighter gray. The wings were sparrow-like brownish with faint wing-bars. Some brownish extended onto the back. The males had black surrounding the bill above and below. The bill was pinkish-orange. The female had a more extensive black chin and lacked black above the bill. The bill was pinkish-orange and somewhat darker tipped. The legs were black.Behaviors: The males sang from the top of shrubs with exposed dead snags, then flew long distances (100yards) and typically disappeared from sight for long periods. The flight was generally low.  The birds were hard to approach for close looks. Even when singing. Pattern of movement was not predictable, although the males did return to the same singing perches with some frequency although the interval could be one to two hours.Call: Three or four clear beginning notes transitioning into a descending thrill. To me it was similar to a Field SparrowPlumage: Alternate
photographer: Photographer: Jim Beatty, Jim Beatty, Jim Beatty
